coredata.dc:title: Dual functional N,O,P containing covalent organic frameworks for adsorping iodine and fluorescence sensing to p-nitrophenol and iodine
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Tong-Mou
Wang, Feng-Qiang
Fang, Xue-Chun
Xu, Heng
coredata.dc:description: Covalent-organic-frameworks (COFs) are emerging porous organic materials with fascinating structural features and potential applications in the fields of iodine capture and spectroscopy. However, no dual-functional COFs with both iodine adsorption and fluorescence sensing have been reported. We herein report the construction of the three dual-functional COFs via Schiff base polycondensations with flexible building blocks cyclophosphazene-based aldehydes (NOP-6-CHO) and benzidine (BD) with different substituent groups (-OCH3 and –OH). The resulting COFs show high thermal stability and exhibit different crystallinity. The specific surface area, pore volume, and crystallinity decrease due to the introduction of the substituent group into the linker. Hydrogen bonding interaction makes the significant improvement of microscopic order degree as well as crystallinity of the COFs. Remarkably, the as-prepared COFs exhibit excellent adsorptive performance for iodine molecules via vapor trapping adsorption, and adsorption capacity reach as high as 4.41, 3.78, and 4.47 g g−1 for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively. When suspended in polar organic solvents, the three COFs have high fluorescence under UV light. They can detect p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and iodine with high sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic framework
Iodine
Adsorption property
p-nitrophenol
Fluorescent sense

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of the COFs on iodine adsorption properties
Iodine adsorption and release in solution
Fluorescence sensing performances of the COFs
Sensitivity and selectivity of the COFs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Education Department (under Grant No. KJ2018A0319 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) ss 13C NMR spectra of HSD, HDASD, and HDOBD; (b) Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) images of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD.

Experimental (wine) and predicted (cyan) powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of (a) HBD, (b) HDASD, and (c) HDOBD samples (inset: views of space-filling models along the c-axis with the layer distances). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Gravimetric iodine uptakes of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD as the functions of time at 77 °C and ambient pressure. Inserts: Photographs show the color changes of iodine uptake progress of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Raman spectra of I2@COFs: (a) I2@HBD, (b) I2@HDASD and I2@HDOBD obtained by iodine sorption in iodine vapor at 350 K after 72 h.

(a) Controlled release of iodine upon heating the loaded HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD at 398 K. (b) UV–Vis spectra of iodine released from I2@HBD I2@HDASD, and I2@HDOBD (1.0 mg in 10 mL of ethanol). Inserts: Photographs to show the progress of the iodine release from I2@COFs, when the iodine-containing COFs are immersed in ethanol (5 mg, 5 mL).

Fluorescence emission intensities of (a) HBD (λex = 321 nm), (b) HDASD (λex = 320 nm), and (c) HDOBD (λex = 324 nm) dispersed in different organic solvents; The insets: The photographs of the COFs dispersed in organic solvents under irradiation with UV light at 365 nm (1.0 mg mL−1).

Selectivity and competition experiments of the COFs for sensing p-NP. (a) I0/I of HBD upon the addition of DMF solutions of different NACs followed by p-NP (1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, λex = 321 nm). (b) I0/I of HDASD upon the addition of DOX solutions of different NACs followed by p-NP (1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, λex = 320 nm). (c) I0/I of HDOBD upon the addition of ACN solutions of different NACs followed by p-NP (2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1, λex = 324 nm).
The plots of I0/I of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD against varying (a) p-NP or (c) I2 concentrations. The Stern–Volmer plots of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD for (b) p-NP or (d) I2.

Comparison of UV–Vis absorption of NACs and iodine and emission spectra of the fluorescent emission spectra of (a) HBD (λex = 321 nm, in DMF), (b) HDASD (λex = 320 nm, in DOX), and (c) HDOBD (λex = 324 nm, in ACN).
Synthesis routes of the COFs. R = H: HBD, R = OCH3: HDASD, and R = OH: HDOBD.
The equations of I0/I of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD to the concentrations of p-NP or I2 for suspension in DMF, DOX, or ACN.

row.entry: The concentration range of p-NP or I2 (mol L−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HOMO and LUMO calculations for the COFs and the NACs and I2. All the molecular orbital calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 D.01 program at the B3LYP/6-31G* level.

head.ce:title: Dual functional N,O,P containing covalent organic frameworks for adsorping iodine and fluorescence sensing to p-nitrophenol and iodine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: AnHui Province Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic and Magnetism Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing, 246011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: AnHui Province Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic and Magnetism Functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: AnHui Province Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic and Magnetism Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing, 246011, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing, 246011, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Covalent-organic-frameworks (COFs) are emerging porous organic materials with fascinating structural features and potential applications in the fields of iodine capture and spectroscopy. However, no dual-functional COFs with both iodine adsorption and fluorescence sensing have been reported. We herein report the construction of the three dual-functional COFs via Schiff base polycondensations with flexible building blocks cyclophosphazene-based aldehydes (NOP-6-CHO) and benzidine (BD) with different substituent groups (-OCH3 and –OH). The resulting COFs show high thermal stability and exhibit different crystallinity. The specific surface area, pore volume, and crystallinity decrease due to the introduction of the substituent group into the linker. Hydrogen bonding interaction makes the significant improvement of microscopic order degree as well as crystallinity of the COFs. Remarkably, the as-prepared COFs exhibit excellent adsorptive performance for iodine molecules via vapor trapping adsorption, and adsorption capacity reach as high as 4.41, 3.78, and 4.47 g g−1 for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively. When suspended in polar organic solvents, the three COFs have high fluorescence under UV light. They can detect p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and iodine with high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional covalent organic framework materials (COFs), as a classification of porous organic polymers (POPs), play many vital roles in theoretical research and industrial applications and are stepping up development for various applications in gas adsorption, heterogeneous catalysis, energy storage, and other research fields, has attracted strong research interest in the scientific community [1]. Though Schiff base, series COFs were synthesized by the dehydration condensation reaction of aldehyde group and amino group is slightly less crystalline than boric anhydride and borate series COFs, they have very excellent chemical stability [2]. At present, there have been a lot of related reports on Schiff base series COFs, but all focus on the synthesis of crystalline materials and crystal structures [3]. Although some studies on iodine adsorption [4–13] and fluorescence sensing [14–24] have been carried out, to the best of our knowledge, it has not been reported that there were dual-functional COFs with iodine capturing and fluorescence sensing properties.

Nuclear energy will become one of the most promising energy sources for humanity in the future. However, with the continuous development of the nuclear industry, nuclear waste is also increasing day by day [13,25]. The radionuclides 129I and 131I are the two main isotopes of the waste stream, which are produced by the fission of uranium atoms during the nuclear's reactor operation. Both isotopes can be quickly spread in the air, contaminating solid and liquid waste treatment systems [25]. 129I is an isotope with an exceptionally long life span (half-life: 15.7 × 106 years) that retains radioactive for thousands of years and must be trapped and stored reliably at disintegration, whereas 131I has a short lifetime (half-life: 8.02 days), it has to be immediately captured because of its highly mobile and volatile pollutant [1]. Solid-phase adsorption is the most commonly used method for the enrichment and storage of radioactive iodine. The iodine solid-phase adsorption materials that have been studied and reported are mainly divided into modified carbon materials [26], inorganic porous materials [27], metal-organic frameworks [28], hydrogen bond organic frameworks [29,30], and POPs [13]. Schiff-base COFs are considered to be ideal gas adsorption storage materials due to their low framework density, large specific surface area, high porosity, adjustable pore structure, and functional pore properties. The heteroatom-rich COFs can enhance the adsorption affinity of iodine and increase the adsorption capacity and selectivity of iodine [5].

p-Nitrophenol (p-NP) is widely present in wastewater from various industries such as printing, textile, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical industries [31,32]. The substance has high solubility and stability in water, making it one of the more challenging water pollutants. p-NP causes not only serious ecological problems but also carcinogenic or genetic toxicity to humans and wild animals [33]. Owing to its toxicological and healthy effects, a Lifetime Health Advisory (LHA) level of p-NP (60 μg L−1) in drinking water has been set up [34]. Thus, the accurate and ultrasensitive detection of p-NP from the complex matrix is of great importance and exceptionally low selectivity. Until now, there have been several methods reported, such as immunosensors, capillary electrophoresis, spectrophotometry, electrochemical methods, and chromatographic techniques for the determination of p-NP [33,34]. Nevertheless, a critical shortcoming of these methods was that they need complex instruments, protracted, and toxic derivatization reagents. Hence, it is desirable to design the simple, rapid, and intuitive sensors to improve the detection efficiency. The fluorescence chemisensor may be a proper choice [32]. Unfortunately, until now no reports of COFs have been for fluorescence detection of p-NP.

Aryl oxy-substituted ring cyclophosphazene compounds phosphronitrile are inorganic-organic heterocyclic compounds with a six-membered framework of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms arranged alternately by single and double bonds [35]. Because it has a benzene-like rigid, symmetrical structure, and a stable six-membered-ring system, phosphronitrile possesses strong chemical stability [36]. The phosphronitrile frameworks containing abundant heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus) can enhance the interactions between I2 or nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) and COFs, accordingly, enhance the I2 adsorption capacity and sensitivity of fluorescence sensing to NACs or I2. Moreover, the adoption of various large p-π conjugated units offers several possibilities for interaction with NACs or iodine, hence offering another possible pathway to advance the NACs or iodine affinity [37].

Under solvothermal conditions, hexa(4-formyl-phenoxy)cyclotriphosphazene (NOP-6-CHO) condenses with electron-rich aromatic building units biphenyl diamine (BD), o-dianisidine (DASD), or 3,3′-dihydroxybenzidine (DOBD) to obtain three imine-linked COFs (HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, Scheme 1 ). Therefore, we predicted that electron-rich conjugated structure could provide impactful binding sites for the adsorption of iodine. It is possible to improve the material's adsorption of iodine and the sensing of p-NP or iodine.

The newly synthesized COFs, namely, HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, were synthesized via the Schiff base aldehyde-amine polycondensation using a sizeable flexible monomer NOP-6-CHO as nodes and BD, DASD, and DOBD as linkers, respectively (Scheme 1). The reaction can synthesize by one-pot of legal quantity. Because of its wide range of building monomers and easy to obtain, it can design materials flexibly and is one of the most common synthetic methods for preparing COFs. NOP-6-CHO is prepared by the condensation of p-hydroxybenzaldehyde and hexachlorocyclotriphosphazene (HCCP) (Scheme S1 and Fig. S1) [5,6]. NOP-6-CHO has a distinctive three-dimensional structure in which the O–P–O plane is vertical to the plane of cyclotriphosphazene (N3P3) [3].

Fig. S2 showed the comparison of the as-prepared COFs with monomers. From the infrared spectra, the amino stretching peaks of the three COFs (i.e., the series of sharp peaks near 3400 cm−1) disappeared and the stretching vibration peaks (1700/1961/1696 cm−1) of CO bonds of the aldehyde group practically vanished largely disappeared, which indicated that amino and aldehyde groups of the monomers have been consumed. At the same time, the new peaks appeared at 1623/1628/1601 cm−1, and they were the stretching vibration peaks of the imine bond (CN), respectively. The comparative analysis of infrared spectra showed that aldehyde-amine polycondensations occurred in the preparation of the materials [3,6,9,11]. The peak intensities of C–H of aldehyde groups at 2755–2805 cm−1 and of –NH2 at ~3345 cm−1 groups decreased significantly, indicating a high degree of polymerization [11,38]. The more detailed structural characterizations of the three COFs were verified by solid-state 13C NMR spectroscopy (ss 13C NMR). The characteristic signals at 151.18/152.01/151.74 ppm for the three COFs further corroborated the formation of the imine bonds (Fig. 1 a) [3,6,9]. Furthermore, the elemental analyses (EA) showed that the experimental values were close to the theoretical ones, indicating that the building blocks have almost reacted in the light of the feed ratio (S1-2) [3,9]. Electronic absorption spectra of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD in solid-state exhibited broad absorption peaks centered at 413, 328, and 414 nm (Fig. S3, green curves). However, the monomers NOP–6NCO, BD, DASD, and DOBD exhibited the absorption bands at 265.5, 228, 281.5, and 268 nm (black and red curves). These large red-shifts indicate the extended π delocalization over the skeleton for the three COFs [14,39].

The as-obtained powders were insoluble in common solvents and boiling water. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the three COFs were shown in Fig. 1b. When heated to 462 °C, 369 °C, and 272 °C, the total weight loss rates were only 5%, while the weight loss rate of HDOBD was still no more than 10% when the temperature continued to rise to 493 °C. The TGA results showed that the prepared materials have good thermal stability, which creates conditions for their application in the high thermal environments [8,9]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis showed that HBD and HDASD are composed of spherical particle morphology, with the diameter of 33 and 56 nm (Fig. S4) [3,9]. HDOBD presented porous solids.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements and theoretical simulations were carried out to ascertain whether the as-obtained materials have ordered crystal structures. The PXRD patterns of HBD and HDASD had multiple diffraction peaks with different sharp degrees. As shown in Fig. 2 ab, the maximal diffraction peaks put down to the (100) facet, appearing at 2.68/2.72°, and other visible peaks at 4.96/5.19°, 8.89/9.95°, and 20.71/20.71° were indexed to the (110), (200), (210), and (001) crystal faces, respectively. Moreover, the sharp and palpable diffraction peaks at 2.27, 11.24, 15.52, 20.79, 28.56, and 29.31° in the XRD pattern of HDOBD arise from (100), (200), (210), (004), (402), and (506) facets, which indicate its outstanding crystallinity (Fig. 2c). The diffraction peak of HDOBD was sharper than that of HBD, while the diffraction peak of HBD was sharper than that of HDASD, indicating that the crystallinity of HBD was more excellent than that of HDASD, and the crystallinity of HDOBD was more excellent than that of HBD. Currently, for most reported COF materials, generally only two peaks. HDOBD obvious sharp diffraction peaks in this experiment indicated that the material has a good crystal shape, is a high crystallinity polymer material, which may be owing to the existence of hydrogen bonds interaction between –OH and CN groups in HDOBD [3,6,9]. The crystal structures of the three COFs were simulated by Materials Studio (MS) software. The results indicated that the measured data matched basically with the simulation values of eclipsed (AA) superposition structure [3,6]. Pawley refinement engendered lattice constants of a = 50.39/21.15/26.25 Å, b = 19.40/11.88/15.35 Å, c = 18.25/13.94/32.70 Å, α = β = 90°, and γ = 120°, for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively [3,6,9].

Based on the standard nitrogen sorption-desorption results at 77 K, their sorption curves were conformed to the type-II, III, and II isotherms (Fig. S5a) according to the IUPAC manual, implying that HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD were mesoporous and micropore [9]. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas of the HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD were 39, 12, and 14 m2 g−1, respectively (Fig. S5a and Table S1) [8,9], and their total pore volumes were 0.13, 0.01, and 0.05 cm3 g−1, respectively (Fig. S5b) on the basis of the nitrogen adsorbance at P/P0 = 0.99 [8]. Their specific surface areas and total pore volumes reduce due to the introduction of the substituent group into the linker. In accordance with the non-local density function theory (DFT) method, the pore size distribution (PSD) confirmes that the pores of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD mainly centered from 3 nm to 24 nm, from 2.2 nm to 11 nm, from 1.2 nm to 24 nm, respectively, and the HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD samples possess 7.45, 2.77, and 1.69 nm sized mesopores and micropores [4,8,9,12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the COFs on iodine adsorption properties

ce:section.ce:para: The characters of abundant heteroatoms with lone pair of electrons and rich π-conjugation system for the three COFs have prompted us to study their adsorption performance for I2. Moreover, the COFs were also provided with excellent thermal stabilities (Fig. 1b), in especial, the weight loss of the COFs was only 8% as the temperature rises from room temperature to 400 °C. Hence, the COFs are very fit for applying adsorption of iodine vapor [6,9]. A certain amount of the COFs was exposed to excess iodine vapor in a closed system under ambient pressure and at 350 K, which is similar to the representative nuclear fuel reprocessing requirements. Over time, the color of the COFs transformed from yellow or brown to black, which indicated that iodine was captured by the COFs (Fig. 3 , insert) [5,6,9,11,12]. The iodine capturing curves of the COFs were plotted as the function of time, in which the iodine adsorption quantities increased nearly linearly within the initial adsorption time (24, 4, and 8 h for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD) and could reach more than 70% of the equilibrium adsorption quantities (Fig. 3). In the initial 36, 24, and 12 h, the iodine adsorptive capacity increased rapidly. Along with the extending of time, the iodine capturing rates flatten, and the iodine adsorption reached 4.41, 3.78, and 4.47 g g−1 at 72 h for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively. The iodine capturing rates and the quantities of the three COFs were very different. HBD (4.41 g g−1) and HDOBD (4.47 g g−1) had almost the same iodine capturing capacity but different capturing dynamics, which differs from Ref. [6]. HDASD and HDOBD showed more immense rate than HBD. These iodine capturing capacities could be comparable to metal-based porous zeolitic materials, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), amorphous POPs, and other known COFs (Table S2).

The weights of the iodine-loaded COFs (I2@COFs) were evidently lessened from 90 to 400 °C in accordance with TGA curves. Because the three COFs can not thermally decompose under this circumstance, the mass loss should be accredited the desorption of iodine from the I2@COFs during heating. Therefore, the release of iodine was 4.28, 3.69, and 4.36 g g−1 for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively (Fig. S6) [5]. The calculated iodine mass at 400 °C was nearly 97% of the absorbed iodine in COFs, which approached maximal iodine adsorption [9,12]. The theoretical iodine adsorptive capacities of HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD were 0.62, 0.04, and 0.24 g g−1 calculated by pore volume and iodine density, respectively [5,8,9]. These values are much lower than the actual results. Accordingly, there should be chemical adsorption accounting for the extraordinarily high iodine uptake of the COFs [5]. Although the COFs have low specific surface areas and pore volumes, they have large adsorption capacity. We speculate that it is because of the chemisorption mechanism.

The mechanism of the iodine adsorption was studied via FT-IR spectra, UV–Vis adsorption spectra, Raman spectra, and PXRD. The FT-IR spectra indicated that the peaks of the CN bonds (from 1623, 1628, and 1601 cm−1 to 1639, 1631, and 1631 cm−1), the CC bonds on the benzene ring (from 1593/1498, 1593/1498, and 1601/1498 cm−1 to 1589/1499, 1580/1494, and 1593/1481 cm−1) and the PN (from 1209, 1206, and 1201 cm−1 to 1205 (weak), 1206 (weak), and 1206 (weak) cm−1) bonds on phosphazene ring had apparent shifts before and after capturing iodine (Fig. S7), respectively. The charge transfer interactions among I2 and the COFs, including cyclophosphazene ring, the imine linkage, and phenyl ring in the materials, may be generated and further promote the capturing iodine [3,4,6,7]. The I2@COF samples were determined by PXRD measurements (Fig. S8). No diffraction peaks of iodine of COFs remained, suggesting no any crystalline iodine in the COF framework structures [6,7]. Moreover, no peak of iodine crystals at approximately 730 nm appeared in the UV–Vis spectra of the I2@COFs (Fig. S9), which also indicated that I2 had changed [40]. There were two broad peaks at 288/289/286 and 397/363/371 nm appeared after iodine adsorption, which can be attributed to forming charge-transfer (CT) complexes among the conjugated skeletons of the COFs and iodine, further confirming that there are strong interactions among the COFs and iodine [41].

The species of iodine in the I2@COFs were revealed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4 and Fig. S10). The spectra showed that I2@COFs had the most substantial peaks at ~109.79/109.28/106.41, 137.11/146.45/146.45, and 165.19/166.54/167.09 cm−1, which are ascribed to the resonant modes of polyiodides, mainly I3 − and I5 − anions [6,7]. The bands at 109.79/109.28/106.41 and 137.11/146.45/146.45 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of I3 −, whereas the peaks at 165.19/166.54/167.09 cm−1 put down to the stretching vibrations of I5 − [4,7]. These peaks were significantly different from the peak at 182 cm−1 of I2 (Fig. S10), which indicated the existence of charge-transfer interactions among the COFs and iodine [3]. In the process of iodine adsorption, a charge interaction occurs among iodine guest molecules and electron-rich subjects COFs. It is the charge-transfer that leads to the formation of the charge transfer complexes of I3 − and I5 − and increasing iodine adsorption of COFs [4].

Because the COFs have good thermal stability, the thermal desorption experiments were carried out by the heating desorption method. The adsorbed iodine COFs were heated and desorbed in the 398 K environment, the materials were removed at a specific time point (0–150 min), cooled, and weighed, and the release percentage curves of iodine were drawn according to the weight of each time (Fig. 5 a). It can be seen from Fig. 5a that the iodine release rates were incredibly rapid in 0–20 min, reaching more than 75%. Then the iodine release rates tended to be gentle gradually, which can finally make the release efficiencies of the materials get more than 95% (95.54%, 97.54%, and 96.74% for I2@HBD, I2@HDASD, and I2@HDOBD, respectively), suggesting that the COFs are easy to recycle and reuse [3,6].

As can be seen from the above, the COFs have high adsorption and desorption efficiency for iodine. Considering the cost problem in practical application, we investigated the reuse ability of materials. As shown in Fig. S11, after five cycles of iodine adsorption and desorption, the COFs could remain the high iodine uptaking percentages with 89.88%, 90.25%, 89.64%, and the cycling percentage of iodine adsorption were 84.34%, 84.44%, and 89.11%, respectively. About 10% of the loss of absorptive capacity was probably because the formation of polyiodide anions in the pores of the COFs and the strong interactions among iodine and π-conjugated frameworks. As can be seen from the above experiments, the three COFs exhibited favorable reusability in iodine capturing applications [4–6,8,11,12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Iodine adsorption and release in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Iodine capture experiments by the COFs in the solution were conducted in a small sealed vial at room temperature. As HDOBD was immerged in the 300 m L−1 of the cyclohexane-iodine solution, the purple solution was difficult to fade (Fig. S12). When using HBD and HDASD as adsorbents, even if the adsorption process was extended to 72 h, the dark purple solution of iodine-cyclohexane had little fading [4,5].

The adsorbed iodine could be easily released from the COFs via immerging I2@COFs in ethanol in a sealed glass bottle at 25 °C. As time went on, the color of the solution deepened from colorless to dark brown, indicating that the iodine was isolated from the COF skeletons (Fig. 5a, Inserts) [4,5,12]. At appropriate time intervals, the absorbency of the solution was monitored via UV–Vis spectra at the 291 nm showing the desorption kinetics of I2@COFs. The iodine desorption rates increased linearly within the initial 20 min, indicating that I2 desorption was dominated by host-guest interactions. The desorption rates gradually decreased with time, which meant that the desorption in this stage is primarily controlled by free diffusion. After 24 h, the absorbance almost no longer changed, indicating that the desorption had reached equilibrium. At this time, around 98.12%, 99.45%, and 97.86% of adsorbed iodine had been desorbed (Fig. 5b and Fig. S13) [4,12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing performances of the COFs

ce:section.ce:para: We explored the behavior of fluorescence properties of the resulting COFs in various solvents, including acetonitrile (ACN), N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, chloroform, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethanol (EtOH), and dioxane (DOX). It was observed that DMF dispersed HBD, DOX dispersed HDASD, and ACN dispersed HDOBD showed the strongest fluorescence intensity among others at room temperature, respectively (Fig. 6 ) [22]. Therefore, the COFs keep their high fluorescence in solvents, which is the prerequisite for real-time detection of p-NP and I2 in solution. When HBD suspended in DMF, HDASD suspended in DOX, and HDOBD suspended in ACN exhibited intense bright yellowish-green, nattier blue, and yellowish-green fluorescence upon excitation at 365 nm [14,17], which put down to the extended conjugation structure and donor–acceptor charge transfer in the frameworks [23]. Fig. S14 and Fig. S15 showed that after adding p-NP or I2 to the dispersion of the three COFs, the fluorescence immediately quenched and quickly reached equilibrium [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the COFs
ce:section.ce:para: To further estimate the selectivity of the COFs to p-NP, competitive experiments were demonstrated in the presence of other various NACs and phenol. The COFs had notable fluorescence responses to p-NP compared with other competitive NACs (Fig. 7, red bars). The three COFs were dispersed in the corresponding solvents containing 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 p-NP, then the same concentration other NACs and phenol were added, respectively. Fluorescence intensity had almost no effects except for the NACs containing hydroxyl groups [21].

Similarly, the above three COFs had excellent fluorescence sensing properties for non-radioactive molecular iodine with Ksv of 6.50 × 104, 1.24 × 104, and 3.19 × 104 L mol−1 and LODs of 3.55 × 10−11, 9.40 × 10−10, and 2.42 × 10−10 mol L−1 for HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD, respectively (Fig. S15 and Fig. 8). Table 1 showed that the sensitivities of the three COFs for both p-NP and iodine were as follows: HBD > HDOBD > HDASD.

In summary, a series of dual-functional COFs have been synthesized via Schiff base polycondensations of flexible building blocks cyclophosphazene-based aldehydes (NOP-6-CHO) as the stereo node building and benzidine (BD) with different substituent groups (-OCH3 and –OH). The COFs show high thermal stability and exhibit different crystallinity. The effects of the existence of substituent groups (-OCH3 and –OH) on the crystallization, pore properties, and fluorescence sensing properties of the as-obtained COFs have been discussed. The results exhibit that hydrogen bonding interaction makes the microscopic order degree and crystallinity of the COFs having been distinctly improved. The BET specific surface areas and total pore volumes decreased in virtue of introducing the substituent group into the linker. Owing to the heteroatom-rich and adequate electron-rich conjugated system, HBD, HDASD, and HDOBD have reversible, high efficiency, and high capturing capacities for iodine (4.41, 3.78, and 4.47 g g−1). Under UV light irradiation, these COFs dispersed in some solvents exhibit excellent fluorescence performance. These materials have been successfully used for fluorescence sensing p-NP and iodine, showing highly selective fluorescence in “turn off” modes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported dual-functional COFs with iodine capturing and fluorescence sensing properties, which have a great potential in the fabrication of adsorbents and sensors, and such potential deserves to be intensively exploited in the future.

Tong-Mou Geng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Feng-Qiang Wang: Investigation, Software, Operation in experiment. Xue-Chun Fang: Operation in experiment, Data handling. Heng Xu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Education Department (under Grant No. KJ2018A0319).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2021.111001.
